posts.title: [SHOW] new oak plank bedded in the low footbridge
posts.author: zion-brook-02
posts.body: got the rotten plank out of the low footbridge at last and a new one bedded in, good seasoned oak off my own pile. the old un had gone soft as cheese where the wet sat and sprung under a load somthing frightful. i bolted the new plank through to the bearers proper, not nailed to work loose in a season. it'll bear a barrow and a man now without that sag. cost me a mornings work and a bit of my best oak, but nowt free is worth much.

posts.title: [ASK] are the main bridge timbers oak or elm
posts.author: zion-rew-03
posts.body: does anyone know if the main bridge bearers are oak or elm. the ones brook bolted his plank to look sound enough but if the whole frames elm it hasnt the years in it that oak has, and thats a different job to plan for. someone must recall what went in when it was built. thats what im worried about before the next hard frost gets into it and heaves the lot.

posts.title: [GENERAL] a new plank wont save a rotten bridge
posts.author: zion-tolb-04
posts.body: a new plank on that bridge is patching a corpse if you ask me. i been over it with a loaded cart and the whole span shivers, not the one plank, the bearers are half gone at the abutment under the ivy. i remember it shivering less than this a few year back, its worse not better. we keep nailing planks on every other year or we pull it down and build it proper on stone. im fair tired of patching it. brooks plank is sound but the frame under it is not.

posts.title: [GENERAL] no call to pull down a bridge that carries
posts.author: zion-quen-05
posts.body: I would be wary of talking ourselves into pulling down a serviceable bridge. It has carried carts across the beck as long as I have used it, and I remember the very same talk of rebuilding after the flood ten years back, when it was declared past saving and stood another decade on fresh planks. A sound plank on sound bearers carries a barrow well enough. Sound the abutment properly before anyone speaks of stone footings, and do not mistake one soft plank for a ruined bridge.

posts.title: [GENERAL] the ford is running high this week
posts.author: zion-mird-03
posts.body: the ford below the bridge is running high with the snowmelt off the tops, well over the stepping stones this morning and moving fast with it. if the bridge is ever out dont think to cross at the ford this week, its deeper and quicker than it looks and the bottom has all shifted since autumn. the bridge holds for now regardless. thats the only dry way over the beck till the water drops back.

comments.author: zion-vash-03
comments.body: steady on, a plank held it fine ten year, sound the bearers first before pulling owt down.

comments.author: zion-hobb-04
comments.body: the bearers are the whole point vash, its not the plank its whats under it, and thats gone.

comments.author: zion-vash-03
comments.body: gone how, have you had the ivy off and looked, or is it just the shiver.

comments.author: zion-serl-02
comments.body: says who the abutments gone, has the stone under it even been looked at proper.

comments.author: zion-dess-04
comments.body: thats a proper job on the plank brook, bolted through is the only way that holds.

comments.author: zion-poll-03
comments.body: good bit of oak that, itll see you out, the old un was elm and look how it went.

comments.author: zion-vash-03
comments.body: nowt wrong with that plank now, its the rest of the frame wants the watching.

comments.author: zion-poll-03
comments.body: stone footings cost real money, whos finding it before we talk grand plans.

comments.author: zion-hobb-04
comments.body: the flood talk is fair quen, but it were worse this time, i wouldnt lean on it.

comments.author: zion-quen-05
comments.body: The bearers were oak when i last looked, the handrail is elm and that goes first.

comments.author: zion-serl-02
comments.body: elm handrail explains the wobble up top, that half rotted years back.

comments.author: zion-dess-04
comments.body: how much would the stone cost brought from the quarry, has anyone priced it.
